mnemonics.title: Bresenham Decision Rule
mnemonics.content: P < 0 → East (E) only; P ≥ 0 → North-East (NE). Think: 'Negative = No vertical change, Non-negative = Add vertical'.

mnemonics.title: DDA Steps Calculation
mnemonics.content: Steps = MAX(|Δx|, |Δy|). Remember: MAX = MAXimum pixels to plot, not MINimum!

mnemonics.title: Bresenham Initial P
mnemonics.content: P₀ = 2Δy - Δx. Remember: '2Y before X' - multiply dy by 2 first, then subtract dx.

acronyms.usage: Line drawing algorithm that uses differential equations and floating-point arithmetic

acronyms.expansion: Named after Jack Bresenham who developed it at IBM in 1962
acronyms.usage: Most efficient integer-based line drawing algorithm
Determines which pixel to plot next in Bresenham's algorithm

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a grid where you're deciding between two adjacent pixels - choose the one closer to the mathematical line
Think of walking from start to end, taking equal-sized steps in the dominant direction, rounding each coordinate to plot pixels
For slope 0-1: increment x by 1 each step; For slope >1: increment y by 1 each step; This keeps pixel spacing uniform

analogies.analogy: Bresenham is like using only whole numbers to measure (like integer arithmetic), while DDA is like using a precise ruler with fractions (floating-point). Whole number measurements are faster and more efficient.
Think of the decision parameter as a 'budget' for how far off you can drift from the ideal line - when the budget runs out (goes negative/positive), you need to step up/down.
Aliasing on a screen is like looking at a smooth road through a chain-link fence - the fence creates artificial steps where there should be smoothness.
